risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After completion of baseline assessments, participants were randomized to one of two treatment groups by blocked randomization with stratification on gender. The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection." Comment: The study explicitly reports blocked randomization with stratification, which are indicators of a proper random allocation process. Although the exact random-number method is not stated, the description is sufficient to support truly random sequence generation under a permissive Low-risk judgment.

Quote: "The random allocation sequence was provided in sealed envelopes by the study statistician (who had no contact with participants) to a staff member not involved in data collection." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment process using sealed envelopes, with the sequence held by a statistician separate from participant contact. Although the paper does not state "opaque" and "sequentially numbered" together, the sealed envelopes plus separation from recruiters/data collectors indicates the upcoming assignment could not be foreseen by the enrolling staff.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The trial was technically single-blinded given that patients were aware of which intervention they were assigned to (CRM or HE). However, participants were masked to the research hypothesis... Investigators and the staff collecting the data were blinded to the treatment status of the participants." Comment: Participants were explicitly not blinded to treatment assignment, and the outcomes assessed (e.g., blood pressure, vascular function, psychosocial and behavioral measures) could plausibly be influenced by participants' and providers' awareness of allocation, so performance bias is high overall despite blinded outcome assessors.

Quote: "Investigators and the staff collecting the data were blinded to the treatment status of the participants." Comment: The main outcomes were assessed by blinded investigators/data collectors. The primary vascular outcome (FMD) was measured with ultrasound and automated edge detection, and key secondary outcomes included lab biomarkers and BP/anthropometry collected in a blinded fashion. Although participants were not blinded, the study explicitly states outcome assessment was blinded, and the most subjective outcomes (questionnaires) were still collected by blinded staff, reducing detection bias.

Quote: "Of the randomized patients, 21 and 23 participants in the CRM and HE groups, respectively, attended the 6-month visit and 19 in each group attended the 12-month visit." Comment: Attrition was substantial: from 33 CRM and 35 HE randomized, only 19/33 (42% loss) and 19/35 (46% loss) remained at 12 months, i.e. total loss exceeded 30% in both arms. Although the paper states "There was no significant difference in drop-out rate between the two randomized groups" and used mixed-effects ITT analysis, the amount of missing outcome data is too large to consider this low risk under Cochrane thresholds, and ITT alone is not sufficient to offset this level of loss.
